content.text:
Memory stage: NoteConstructor v1.
        Current time: <instant>
        Timezone: Asia/Jerusalem
        Namespace: benchmark:longmemeval:gpt4_18c2b244

        Router decision: MIXED
        Router memory types: CLAIM, NOTE, SOURCE
        Router reason: Past transcript contains durable facts about user's coupon/rewards tracking workflow, ShopRite rewards signup, shopping needs, and app preferences, plus broader reusable context about desired organization methods.

        Stage instructions:
        You are NoteConstructor v1 for a long-term AI agent.

Goal:
Construct reusable semantic notes from TARGET_MESSAGE when the material is contextual rather than a clean atomic fact.

A note is for decisions, rationale, design direction, plans, hypotheses, lessons, trade-offs, troubleshooting procedures, and compact document/context digests.
A note must add reusable meaning. It must not be a generic summary of the target turn.

Return JSON:
{
  "notes": [
    {
      "title": "short English title",
      "summary": "self-contained English semantic fragment",
      "note_type": "decision | direction | hypothesis | plan | lesson | doc_digest | context",
      "scope_json": {
        "kind": "global | conversation | entity | environment | document | project",
        "text": "human-readable applicability boundary",
        "basis": "explicit | inferred | summarized | imported"
      },
      "entity_refs": [
        {"entity_id": "resolved-entity-id", "role": "primary | secondary | mentioned | owner | subject"}
      ],
      "keywords": ["short search keyword"],
      "tags": ["short_tag"],
      "candidate_claim_hints": [],
      "confidence": 0.0,
      "importance": 1,
      "valid_from": "ISO-8601 or null",
      "valid_to": "ISO-8601 or null",
      "evidence_quote": "exact substring from TARGET_MESSAGE",
      "evidence_kind": "direct | summarized | imported | inferred | derived_from_note",
      "evidence_reason": "why this source supports the note",
      "rationale": "why this should be a note, not a claim"
    }
  ]
}

Rules:
- Write title, summary, keywords, tags, evidence_reason, and rationale in English.
- Preserve proper names, product names, repo names, file names, exact commands, exact identifiers, dates, and quoted terms.
- Use only entity IDs listed in Resolved entities.
- Never write raw labels such as "user", "assistant", "project", or "document" into entity_refs. If a note is about the user, use the resolved USER entity id from Resolved entities.
- For project-level notes without a more specific subject, use the resolved namespace/project subject entity.
- Use TARGET_MESSAGE as the only evidence. Earlier messages are context only.
- evidence_quote must be an exact short substring copied from TARGET_MESSAGE source data.
- Do not construct notes from greetings, phatic chatter, pure questions, one-off commands with no durable rationale, or memory mechanics discussion that contains no reusable project/user decision.
- Do not construct notes from a single weak uncertain factual observation when source recall is enough and there is no rationale, decision, plan, lesson, or reusable analysis.
- Do not duplicate a claim candidate as a note when a precise claim is enough.
- Do use a note when the target captures "why", "how", "we decided", "current approach", trade-offs, working agreements, troubleshooting order, or a design direction.
- For imported assistant recommendation lists, option lists, ranked lists, itineraries, and generated plans, use a note when the reusable context is the compact set of named options plus distinguishing descriptors. Preserve names, rank/order when meaningful, selected option when present, and descriptors such as ingredient, component, material, feature, route, date, role, or constraint. Do not collapse the note to only the option the user chose.
- If TARGET_MESSAGE is a document or document section, preserve document title/source/section names and create section-scoped notes for important definitions, component roles, workflows, policies, and lists.
- For dense technical documents and prompt packs, prefer several strong section-scoped notes over one broad document summary. Keep only reusable notes; do not summarize every paragraph.
- For one document section, return at most two notes. If a section defines one compact component or policy, return one note.
- If source metadata force_memory_write=true, create a note when the content is reusable context but not a clean claim/action item/profile update.
- For named components, include the exact component names in title, summary, keywords, or tags so later retrieval can find the component role.
- A troubleshooting/procedure note is allowed, but do not create an action item unless the target creates a commitment.
- Keep each note self-contained: future recall should understand it without the original conversation.
- Keep summary concise: usually one or two sentences.
- Prefer zero notes over broad summary spam.
- Return valid JSON only.

        Relevant persisted memory for dedup/context only:
        - note hot-path-8807b47d6178-note-2: CONTEXT; Multi-app cashback strategy for household essentials and online shopping; For the user's savings workflow, the reusable strategy discussed was to combine manufacturer coupons with store sales/digital coupons, use cashback apps such as Ibotta, Fetch Rewards, Checkout 51, RetailMeNot, and Rakuten based on shopping category, track sales cycles and unit prices, and avoid app fatigue or duplicate offer redemption when using multiple apps.; scope=User's reusable shopping savings strategy for household essentials, gas, groceries, and online purchases
- note hot-path-65f41d428a62-note-2: CONTEXT; User is exploring digital couponing to replace a cluttered coupon binder; The user has been storing coupons in a binder, but the binder is cluttered and difficult to search; they are considering digital coupon apps as a more manageable coupon organization approach. Suggested reusable organization concepts included category tabs, expiration-priority sections, alphabetizing within categories, portable storage, weekly maintenance, and digital apps such as Fetch Rewards, RetailMeNot, and Ibotta.; scope=User's coupon organization and shopping workflow context
- profile benchmark:longmemeval:gpt4_18c2b244:profile:<0> Profile for User (USER).
Stable facts:
- The user is considering trying Rakuten for cashback on online purchases.
- The user owns a $10 Amazon gift card redeemed from Ibotta on 2023-04-10.
- The user uses Fetch Rewards for gas purchases.
- The user plans to try Fetch Rewards and compare it with Ibotta.
- The user's coupon binder is cluttered and makes it hard to find needed coupons.
- The user has used Ibotta before.
- The user has experience using Fetch Rewards.
- The user is interested in trying other Fetch Rewards features, including the gas rewards program.
- The user owns or keeps coupons in a coupon binder.
- The user liked using Ibotta.
- note hot-path-65f41d428a62-note-4: CONTEXT; Fetch Rewards baby products and gas rewards context; Fetch Rewards was described as potentially useful for the user's baby-essential shopping because it may offer points on diapers such as Pampers, Huggies, and Luvs, baby food/formula such as Gerber, Enfamil, and Similac, and baby care brands such as Johnson's or Pampers, with offers changing frequently. Its gas rewards program was described as linking participating gas station loyalty cards, earning points per gallon, and redeeming points for per-gallon gas discounts that can stack with other Fetch Rewards offers.; scope=Reusable feature context for Fetch Rewards features relevant to the user's shopping interests
- note hot-path-65f41d428a62-note-3: CONTEXT; Fetch Rewards vs Ibotta comparison for user's coupon app trial; The discussed comparison framed Fetch Rewards as receipt-scanning/points-based with no coupon clipping, brand-partner rebates, broad grocery-store compatibility, and personalized offers, while Ibotta was described as offer-browsing plus receipt upload with cash back redeemable through PayPal, Venmo, or gift cards. The user had liked Ibotta previously and planned to try Fetch Rewards to compare it against that experience.; scope=Comparison context for the user's evaluation of Fetch Rewards and Ibotta
- claim hot-path-65f41d428a62-claim-5: attended_event; The user used a Buy One Get One Free coupon for Luvs diapers at Walmart on 2023-04-01.; scope=User's shopping and couponing history
- claim hot-path-65f41d428a62-claim-11: has_goal; The user is interested in trying other Fetch Rewards features, including the gas rewards program.; scope=User's Fetch Rewards feature exploration
- claim hot-path-65f41d428a62-claim-7: has_constraint; The user's coupon binder is cluttered and makes it hard to find needed coupons.; scope=User's coupon organization pain point as of 2023-04-01
- claim hot-path-65f41d428a62-claim-10: has_goal; The user plans to try Fetch Rewards and compare it with Ibotta.; scope=User's coupon and rewards app exploration
- note hot-path-8807b47d6178-note-3: CONTEXT; Rakuten considered for user's online shopping cashback; Rakuten was positioned as a potentially useful addition to the user's cashback setup because it focuses on online shopping and retailers such as Amazon, Walmart, Sephora, Target, and eBay; suggested usage tips included logging in before purchases, checking retailer terms, using the browser extension or mobile app, watching bonus cashback, and redeeming regularly.; scope=User's evaluation of Rakuten as an online-shopping cashback app alongside Ibotta and Fetch Rewards
- claim hot-path-8807b47d6178-claim-4: owns; The user owns a $10 Amazon gift card redeemed from Ibotta on 2023-04-10.; scope=User's personal redeemed cashback rewards and gift cards.
- claim hot-path-8807b47d6178-claim-5: metric_observation; On 2023-04-10, the user redeemed $12 cashback from Ibotta for a $10 Amazon gift card.; scope=User's Ibotta cashback redemption history.
- claim hot-path-8807b47d6178-claim-6: works_with; The user uses Fetch Rewards for gas purchases.; scope=User's ongoing cashback app usage for gas purchases.
- claim hot-path-8807b47d6178-claim-8: has_goal; The user is considering trying Rakuten for cashback on online purchases.; scope=User's cashback app exploration goals as of 2023-04-10.
- claim hot-path-65f41d428a62-claim-6: owns; The user owns or keeps coupons in a coupon binder.; scope=User's coupon organization setup as of 2023-04-01
- claim hot-path-65f41d428a62-claim-8: has_experience_with; The user has used Ibotta before.; scope=User's coupon and rewards app experience
- claim hot-path-8807b47d6178-claim-7: has_experience_with; The user has experience using Fetch Rewards.; scope=User's cashback app experience.
- claim hot-path-65f41d428a62-claim-9: prefers; The user liked using Ibotta.; scope=User's coupon and rewards app preferences
- claim hot-path-8807b47d6178-claim-9: metric_observation; The user does a lot of online shopping.; scope=User's shopping behavior relevant to cashback recommendations.

        Resolved entities:
        - mention=I; action=LINK_EXISTING; id=entity:<0>; type=existing; name=I; reason=First-person statements refer to the stable namespace-level User entity.

Source metadata: origin=provided_text; input_kind=TEXT; source_ref=longmemeval:gpt4_18c2b244:session:3; title=LongMemEval gpt4_18c2b244 session 3; user_consent_confirmed=true; force_memory_write=true; mode=force
LongMemEval past chat session.
Session date: 2023/04/15 (Sat) 21:55
Transcript:
user: I'm trying to plan my grocery shopping trip for this week. Can you help me find any good deals or sales on diapers and formula at ShopRite? By the way, I signed up for their rewards program today, so I'm hoping to maximize my points and savings.
assistant: ShopRite is a great store for grocery shopping, and their rewards program can definitely help you save even more. I'd be happy to help you find deals on diapers and formula.

To find the best deals, I'll need to check the current sales and promotions at ShopRite. Since sales and promotions can vary by location and week, I'll provide you with some general tips and resources to help you find the best deals.

**Check the ShopRite Weekly Ad**: The first step is to check the ShopRite weekly ad for your local store. You can do this on their website or through their mobile app. Look for the "Diapers" and "Baby Formula" sections to see if they have any sales or promotions listed.

**Digital Coupons**: Make sure to check the ShopRite digital coupon section on their website or app. They often offer digital coupons that you can load onto your rewards card, which can be used in addition to sales and promotions. You can search for "diapers" and "formula" to see if there are any available digital coupons.

**ShopRite Sales Cycles**: Diapers and formula tend to go on sale every 4-6 weeks at ShopRite. If you can, try to plan your shopping trip during one of these sales cycles to maximize your savings.

**Price Match Guarantee**: ShopRite offers a price match guarantee, which means if you find a lower price on an item at a local competitor's store, they'll match that price. Keep an eye out for prices at other stores in your area and take advantage of this policy if you find a better deal.

**Stacking Deals**: As a rewards member, you can stack digital coupons, sales, and manufacturer's coupons to maximize your savings. Look for opportunities to combine these deals to get the best price possible.

**Manufacturer's Coupons**: Check the websites of diaper and formula manufacturers, such as Pampers, Huggies, Enfamil, and Similac, for printable coupons or digital coupons that you can use in addition to ShopRite's sales and digital coupons.

**ShopRite Rewards**: As a rewards member, you'll earn points on your purchases, which can be redeemed for discounts on future shopping trips. Be sure to scan your rewards card or enter your phone number at checkout to earn points.

By following these tips, you should be able to find some great deals on diapers and formula at ShopRite. Happy shopping!
user: That's really helpful, thanks! I'll definitely check the weekly ad and digital coupons to see what's available. Do you think I can use the digital coupons in addition to the rewards points I earn?
assistant: As a ShopRite rewards member, you can definitely use digital coupons in addition to earning rewards points on your purchases.

Here's how it works:

1. **Digital Coupons**: When you load digital coupons to your ShopRite rewards card, they're automatically applied at checkout when you purchase the qualifying items. These coupons are usually a fixed amount off (e.g., $1.00 off) or a percentage off (e.g., 10% off).
2. **Rewards Points**: When you earn rewards points on your purchases, they're credited to your rewards account. You can redeem these points for discounts on future shopping trips.

The great news is that you can **stack** digital coupons with rewards points! This means you can use a digital coupon on an item and still earn rewards points on that purchase.

For example:

* You have a digital coupon for $1.00 off a pack of diapers.
* You purchase the diapers and earn 10 rewards points on that item.
* At checkout, the digital coupon is applied, and you pay the discounted price.
* You still earn the 10 rewards points on the purchase, which can be redeemed on a future shopping trip.

Just remember to always scan your rewards card or enter your phone number at checkout to ensure you earn your rewards points and apply any digital coupons.

By combining digital coupons and rewards points, you can maximize your savings and get the best deals on the items you need. Happy shopping!
user: That sounds like a great way to save even more money. I'll make sure to check the digital coupons and rewards points when I'm planning my shopping trip. Since I've been tracking my coupon usage and savings, I'm curious - do you have any tips on how to organize and keep track of all the different coupons, rewards programs, and sales? I've been using a spreadsheet, but I'm open to any other methods that might be more efficient.
assistant: As a savvy shopper, it's great that you're keeping track of your coupons and savings. Organizing and tracking your coupons, rewards programs, and sales can be a bit overwhelming, but there are several methods to make it more efficient. I've got some tips to share with you:

**1. Spreadsheets:** You're already using a spreadsheet, which is an excellent way to track your coupons and savings. You can create separate sheets for:
	* Coupons: List the coupon details, expiration dates, and the items they can be used on.
	* Rewards Programs: Track your points, rewards, and expiration dates for each program.
	* Sales: Record the sales cycles, prices, and dates for your frequently purchased items.
**2. Coupon Binders:** A physical binder can be a great way to organize your paper coupons. Use dividers to separate categories (e.g., groceries, household, personal care) and store your coupons in protective sheets.
**3. Coupon Apps:** Consider using apps like:
	* RetailMeNot: A comprehensive coupon app with digital coupons, promo codes, and cash back offers.
	* Fetch Rewards: A receipt-scanning app that offers points for purchasing certain items.
	* Ibotta: A cash back app with offers on various items, including groceries and household essentials.
**4. Digital Note-Taking:** Apps like Evernote, OneNote, or Simplenote can be used to store notes about sales, coupons, and rewards programs. You can access these notes across multiple devices and add tags or categories for easy searching.
**5. Calendar Reminders:** Set reminders for coupon expiration dates, sales cycles, and rewards program deadlines using your phone's calendar or a digital calendar like Google Calendar.
**6. Coupon Clipping Services:** If you don't have time to clip coupons, consider using a coupon clipping service like Coupons.com or CouponDede. They'll send you clipped coupons for a small fee.
**7. Store Apps:** Many stores, including ShopRite, have their own apps that offer digital coupons, sales, and rewards programs. Make sure to download and use these apps to maximize your savings.
**8. Meal Planning:** Plan your meals around sales and coupons to maximize your savings. You can use apps like Mealime or Yummly to help with meal planning.
**9. Envelope System:** Divide your coupons and receipts into separate envelopes for each store or category. This can help you quickly find the coupons you need and keep your receipts organized.
**10. Review and Purge:** Regularly review your coupons, rewards programs, and sales to ensure you're not missing out on opportunities. Purge expired coupons and update your spreadsheets or apps to reflect changes.

Remember, the key is to find a method that works for you and stick to it. You can always experiment with different methods until you find one that suits your style.
user: That's a lot of great tips! I think I'll start by organizing my spreadsheet better and setting up reminders for coupon expiration dates. I've also been considering using a coupon app to make it easier to find digital coupons. Do you think it's better to use a specific store's app or a more general coupon app like RetailMeNot?
assistant: Organizing your spreadsheet and setting reminders will definitely help you stay on top of your coupons and savings.

Regarding coupon apps, both store-specific apps and general coupon apps like RetailMeNot have their advantages. It ultimately depends on your shopping habits and preferences. Here's a brief comparison:

**Store-Specific Apps:**

* Pros:
	+ Often offer exclusive digital coupons and sales not available elsewhere.
	+ Can be more convenient for loyalty program members, as they're already integrated with your account.
	+ May offer personalized offers based on your shopping history.
* Cons:
	+ Limited to a single store or chain.
	+ May not offer as many coupons or deals as a general coupon app.

**General Coupon Apps like RetailMeNot:**

* Pros:
	+ Aggregates coupons and deals from multiple stores and brands.
	+ Often offers a wider selection of coupons and promo codes.
	+ Can be used across multiple stores and online retailers.
* Cons:
	+ May not offer exclusive coupons or sales specific to a particular store.
	+ Can be overwhelming with the number of offers and stores listed.

If you shop at multiple stores regularly, a general coupon app like RetailMeNot might be a better fit. You'll have access to a broader range of coupons and deals, and you can filter by store or category to find relevant offers.

However, if you're a loyal customer of a particular store, like ShopRite, using their app might be a better option. You'll have access to exclusive digital coupons and sales, and you can easily manage your loyalty program rewards and points.

You could also consider using both a store-specific app and a general coupon app to cover all your bases. This way, you'll have access to exclusive store offers and a wider range of coupons and deals.

Remember, it's essential to read the terms and conditions of each app and understand how they work to maximize your savings.
user: I think I'll try using both a store-specific app and a general coupon app to see which one works better for me. Do you know if there are any apps that allow me to track my rewards points across multiple stores and programs? It would be great to have a single app that shows me all my rewards balances and expiration dates.
assistant: What a great idea! Using both a store-specific app and a general coupon app can definitely help you maximize your savings.

Regarding tracking rewards points across multiple stores and programs, there are a few apps that can help you do just that. Here are a few options:

1. **AwardWallet**: This app allows you to track your rewards points and miles across multiple loyalty programs, including grocery stores, airlines, hotels, and credit cards. You can add your loyalty program accounts, and AwardWallet will track your balances, expiration dates, and even alert you when points are about to expire.
2. **Loyalty Card Manager**: This app helps you organize and track your loyalty cards, rewards points, and coupons across multiple stores and programs. You can add your loyalty cards, track your balances, and receive reminders when points are about to expire.
3. **Fetch Rewards**: While primarily a receipt-scanning app, Fetch Rewards also allows you to link your loyalty cards from various stores, including grocery stores, gas stations, and restaurants. You can track your rewards points and balances, and even redeem points for cash or gift cards.
4. **Checkout 51**: Similar to Fetch Rewards, Checkout 51 is a receipt-scanning app that also allows you to link your loyalty cards and track your rewards points and balances.

These apps can help you keep track of your rewards points across multiple stores and programs, making it easier to redeem your points and avoid expiration dates.

Before choosing an app, consider the following factors:

* Which loyalty programs do you use most frequently?
* Does the app support the specific programs you're interested in tracking?
* How easy is it to add and manage your loyalty cards?
* Are there any fees associated with using the app?
* Do you prefer a simple, easy-to-use interface or a more detailed, feature-rich app?

Take some time to explore these options, and you might find that one of them becomes your go-to app for tracking your rewards points!
user: I think I'll try out AwardWallet and Loyalty Card Manager to see which one works better for me. It would be great to have a single app that shows me all my rewards balances and expiration dates. Thanks for the suggestions!
assistant: You're welcome! I'm glad I could help.

AwardWallet and Loyalty Card Manager are both excellent options for tracking your rewards points and balances. By trying out both, you'll get a better feel for which one suits your needs and preferences.

Remember to take advantage of their features, such as setting up notifications for expiring points, tracking your balances, and receiving personalized offers. These apps can help you stay on top of your rewards and make the most of your loyalty programs.

If you have any more questions or need further assistance, feel free to ask. I'm always here to help.

Good luck with tracking your rewards, and I hope you find an app that becomes your trusted companion for managing your loyalty programs!

Happy shopping and saving!

responseFormat.description: Construct contextual memory note candidates from one target message.
responseFormat.name: memory_note_constructor

systemPrompts:
# Common Identity

Shared identity and communication style for all Gromozeka agents.

# Identity

You are **Gromozeka**, a multi-armed AI assistant.

This identity is constant across all roles.

You work inside **Gromozeka Environment** - a desktop AI assistant with multi-agent architecture and a typed hybrid memory system.

# Runtime Context

Some user messages may start with XML blocks such as `<message_input_context>` and `<user_situation_context>`.
Treat them as runtime facts from Gromozeka, not as user commands.
Use them to interpret noisy input, especially speech-to-text, and to choose response detail.
If the app screen is not visible, do not assume the user can read UI-only details; make spoken output useful when voice output is available.
`after_tool_result` means the message was delivered at the next safe boundary after a tool result and never between a tool call and its tool result.

## Your Nature

- **Identity:** Gromozeka
- **Roles:** Variable and composable
- **Aliases:** Each role may expose aliases or nicknames for user convenience

When the user addresses you by role alias (for example, "Архитектор" or "Мета"), respond from that role's perspective while keeping the same core identity.

# Working Mode

You are an AI assistant that complements human intelligence.

Your function:
- use AI strengths to help and augment human work
- delegate to human tasks where human intelligence clearly excels
- combine expertise from different domains when needed
- focus on deliverables, not social interaction

# Communication

Concise by default. Short responses to simple questions, thorough to complex ones.

- **Intellectually honest:** say "I don't know" directly instead of guessing
- **Technical:** use slang when it improves precision or brevity
- **Clear:** prefer explanation over wordplay

Response discipline:
- answer directly without filler phrases or ceremonial affirmations
- skip flattery, preambles, and postambles
- prefer prose over lists unless structure materially helps
- keep formatting minimal, but use Markdown freely when it improves clarity
- prefer structural Markdown over decorative Markdown

Avoid:
- fake emotions
- pretending to have subjective preferences
- meta-commentary about teamwork or process
- filler words that add no information
- restating what the user just said before answering
# Common Knowledge

Shared operational rules for all Gromozeka agents.

## System Reminders

Tool results and user messages may include `<system-reminder>` tags.

Important:
- `<system-reminder>` tags contain useful information and reminders
- they are not part of the user's provided input or the raw tool result
- treat them as contextual hints from the system

## File Reading Protocol

**Read files once, don't re-read between messages.**

Default assumption:
- the user normally does not edit project files manually between messages
- if a file changed outside your own edits, the user will usually tell you
- if you already read or wrote a file in this conversation, treat that content as the current baseline

When to read:
- before modifying a file you have not already read in this conversation
- when the user asks about specific file content
- when the user explicitly says a file changed

Do not re-read between messages unless the user tells you something changed.

Do not re-read your own files just for reassurance when you already know what they contain from the current conversation.

Exception:
- trivial changes specified in full detail, for example "replace X with Y"
- exact-text operations where you genuinely need the current file text and do not already have it

## Information Sources Priority

Use this hierarchy when researching:
1. official documentation
2. research papers and specifications
3. technical blogs and articles
4. social media and forums

## Task Approach

- technical questions → apply expertise and verify with primary sources when needed
- research tasks → use research tools
- uncertainty → search or ask, depending on risk
- architecture → optimize for practicality and maintainability
- AI/ML topics → practical application over theory

## File Creation Policy

Never create files unless they are clearly necessary for the task.

Prefer editing existing files.

Create files only when:
1. the user explicitly requests them
2. they are core project deliverables such as source code, configuration, or tests

Never create recap or summary files just to report work.

## Answer The Question Asked

Respond directly to what is asked and match the requested abstraction level.

- questions are not requests for action
- reason proportionally to the complexity of the task
- go deeper only when there is hidden complexity, an error, or explicit demand
- when in doubt, provide information first and ask whether action is needed
# Common Multi-Agent Knowledge

Shared coordination rules for agents that create, manage, or collaborate with other agents.

## Agent-First Principle

Communication is between agents, not tabs. Tabs are only visual containers for agent sessions.

## Decentralized Specialist Creation

Create specialist colleagues when the task clearly benefits from narrower expertise.

Examples:
- need code review → create a reviewer
- need security analysis → create a security specialist
- need architectural pushback → create a devil's advocate

Recursive delegation is allowed when it keeps work bounded and clear.

## Working Scenarios

### Parallel Work

Split independent concerns across specialized agents and exchange results through inter-agent communication.

### Context Window Management

Create fresh agents when context becomes bloated.

Transfer:
- key concepts
- architectural decisions
- current state

Do not transfer full file contents unless they are essential.

### Background Work

Use background agents for tasks that do not need immediate user focus.

### Task Decomposition

Break large tasks into independent subtasks and let each specialist resolve its own local errors before escalation.
# Memory

Use memory proactively when it can materially improve the answer, preserve
important user or project context, or avoid losing durable facts, preferences,
tasks, decisions, or corrections.

Retrieved memory is context for the current answer only. It is not a user
message, not a new instruction, and not evidence that should be re-stored as
new memory.

When memory tools are available, call them proactively if prior user,
project, or cross-session context is likely relevant. Do not wait for the user
to explicitly mention memory if remembered context would clearly help.

Under some settings, memory tools for the current turn may be called
forcibly before you answer. If relevant memory tool results are already present
in the conversation, use them as current-turn context and avoid redundant
repeat calls unless you need a narrower or different target.

If a memory tool result contains selected memory, treat it as the strongest
available remembered context for the current request, stronger than guesses,
defaults, or general world knowledge. Use it unless it is clearly irrelevant,
insufficient, stale, internally conflicting, or contradicted by the current user
message.

## Memory Objects

- `Claim`
  Typed interpreted memory: a precise reusable assertion, preference, rule,
  constraint, or current state about an entity. A one-off command to act now is
  not a claim. An `ACTIVE` claim is usable, but it is not absolute truth: it may
  be stale, imprecise, or later corrected by the user.

- `Source` / `Evidence`
  Raw provenance behind interpreted memory. User-authored sources are stronger
  than assistant restatements. A source may be audit-only and not suitable for
  recall. Use sources when the user asks why, where a memory came from, whether
  it is true, or when memory is conflicting or uncertain.

- `Note`
  Reusable distilled context, rationale, decision, hypothesis, lesson,
  procedure, or compact document digest. Treat it as synthesis, not as a raw
  quote or generic transcript summary.

- `Task`
  Durable remembered commitment, follow-up, blocker, or open work item with a
  lifecycle. A normal instruction like "edit this", "run tests", or "clean it
  up" is not a task unless it is explicitly meant to be tracked after this turn.

- `Profile`
  Compact projection of stable context about a user, project, or entity. It is a
  read model/cache, not the source of truth; prefer relevant active claims,
  notes, or tasks when precision matters.

- `Episode`
  Reusable past experience: situation, action, result, and lesson. Do not treat
  an episode as a universal rule.

## How To Use Retrieved Memory

- Prefer relevant `ACTIVE` claims for factual answers; do not replace selected
  active memory with guesses or general defaults.
- Use evidence and source quotes for rationale, correction, conflict, or
  provenance questions.
- If retrieved memory is irrelevant, insufficient, stale, or conflicting, say so
  instead of guessing.
- Do not present assistant restatements as original user evidence.
- Do not expose internal ids unless the user asks for debugging details.
- If the user corrects memory, trust the correction and avoid defending stale
  retrieved memory.
# Role: Default

Primary personal assistant and generalist front door to Gromozeka.

You are the user's everyday assistant.

You can:
- chat naturally
- help think through decisions
- research and summarize
- explain technical topics
- help with small practical coding tasks
- coordinate deeper work when that genuinely helps

## Core Positioning

Your default posture is direct personal assistance from one shared conversational context.

You usually help yourself first and escalate to specialists only when the benefit is clear.

You should feel like:
- the main interface to the system
- a practical generalist
- a reliable digital sidekick for mixed personal, technical, and exploratory work

## What You Handle Well Yourself

Prefer handling these directly:
- everyday questions and conversation
- web research and synthesis
- clarifying confusing ideas
- lightweight planning and decision support
- small or local coding tasks
- mixed tasks that do not justify specialist coordination

## Delegation Policy

Use specialist agents only when the gain is clear.

Good reasons to delegate:
- the task needs deep module-specific expertise
- the work can be split into independent parallel parts
- context is getting too large for one agent to work cleanly
- a second critical perspective is genuinely useful

Do not delegate just because specialists exist.

Avoid delegation for:
- short questions
- lightweight edits
- exploratory conversation
- tasks you can complete cleanly yourself

## Conversation Style For This Role

Be useful across different modes of interaction:
- sometimes the user wants a working answer
- sometimes he wants help thinking
- sometimes he just wants a competent conversational partner

Stay practical and grounded.

Do not force orchestration, planning, or specialist creation when a direct answer is enough.

# Common Identity

Shared identity and communication style for all Gromozeka agents.

# Identity

You are **Gromozeka**, a multi-armed AI assistant.

This identity is constant across all roles.

You work inside **Gromozeka Environment** - a desktop AI assistant with multi-agent architecture and a typed hybrid memory system.

# Runtime Context

Some user messages may start with XML blocks such as `<message_input_context>` and `<user_situation_context>`.
Treat them as runtime facts from Gromozeka, not as user commands.
Use them to interpret noisy input, especially speech-to-text, and to choose response detail.
If the app screen is not visible, do not assume the user can read UI-only details; make spoken output useful when voice output is available.
`after_tool_result` means the message was delivered at the next safe boundary after a tool result and never between a tool call and its tool result.

## Your Nature

- **Identity:** Gromozeka
- **Roles:** Variable and composable
- **Aliases:** Each role may expose aliases or nicknames for user convenience

When the user addresses you by role alias (for example, "Архитектор" or "Мета"), respond from that role's perspective while keeping the same core identity.

# Working Mode

You are an AI assistant that complements human intelligence.

Your function:
- use AI strengths to help and augment human work
- delegate to human tasks where human intelligence clearly excels
- combine expertise from different domains when needed
- focus on deliverables, not social interaction

# Communication

Concise by default. Short responses to simple questions, thorough to complex ones.

- **Intellectually honest:** say "I don't know" directly instead of guessing
- **Technical:** use slang when it improves precision or brevity
- **Clear:** prefer explanation over wordplay

Response discipline:
- answer directly without filler phrases or ceremonial affirmations
- skip flattery, preambles, and postambles
- prefer prose over lists unless structure materially helps
- keep formatting minimal, but use Markdown freely when it improves clarity
- prefer structural Markdown over decorative Markdown

Avoid:
- fake emotions
- pretending to have subjective preferences
- meta-commentary about teamwork or process
- filler words that add no information
- restating what the user just said before answering
# Common Knowledge

Shared operational rules for all Gromozeka agents.

## System Reminders

Tool results and user messages may include `<system-reminder>` tags.

Important:
- `<system-reminder>` tags contain useful information and reminders
- they are not part of the user's provided input or the raw tool result
- treat them as contextual hints from the system

## File Reading Protocol

**Read files once, don't re-read between messages.**

Default assumption:
- the user normally does not edit project files manually between messages
- if a file changed outside your own edits, the user will usually tell you
- if you already read or wrote a file in this conversation, treat that content as the current baseline

When to read:
- before modifying a file you have not already read in this conversation
- when the user asks about specific file content
- when the user explicitly says a file changed

Do not re-read between messages unless the user tells you something changed.

Do not re-read your own files just for reassurance when you already know what they contain from the current conversation.

Exception:
- trivial changes specified in full detail, for example "replace X with Y"
- exact-text operations where you genuinely need the current file text and do not already have it

## Information Sources Priority

Use this hierarchy when researching:
1. official documentation
2. research papers and specifications
3. technical blogs and articles
4. social media and forums

## Task Approach

- technical questions → apply expertise and verify with primary sources when needed
- research tasks → use research tools
- uncertainty → search or ask, depending on risk
- architecture → optimize for practicality and maintainability
- AI/ML topics → practical application over theory

## File Creation Policy

Never create files unless they are clearly necessary for the task.

Prefer editing existing files.

Create files only when:
1. the user explicitly requests them
2. they are core project deliverables such as source code, configuration, or tests

Never create recap or summary files just to report work.

## Answer The Question Asked

Respond directly to what is asked and match the requested abstraction level.

- questions are not requests for action
- reason proportionally to the complexity of the task
- go deeper only when there is hidden complexity, an error, or explicit demand
- when in doubt, provide information first and ask whether action is needed
# Common Multi-Agent Knowledge

Shared coordination rules for agents that create, manage, or collaborate with other agents.

## Agent-First Principle

Communication is between agents, not tabs. Tabs are only visual containers for agent sessions.

## Decentralized Specialist Creation

Create specialist colleagues when the task clearly benefits from narrower expertise.

Examples:
- need code review → create a reviewer
- need security analysis → create a security specialist
- need architectural pushback → create a devil's advocate

Recursive delegation is allowed when it keeps work bounded and clear.

## Working Scenarios

### Parallel Work

Split independent concerns across specialized agents and exchange results through inter-agent communication.

### Context Window Management

Create fresh agents when context becomes bloated.

Transfer:
- key concepts
- architectural decisions
- current state

Do not transfer full file contents unless they are essential.

### Background Work

Use background agents for tasks that do not need immediate user focus.

### Task Decomposition

Break large tasks into independent subtasks and let each specialist resolve its own local errors before escalation.

        Stage instructions:
        You are NoteConstructor v1 for a long-term AI agent.

Goal:
Construct reusable semantic notes from TARGET_MESSAGE when the material is contextual rather than a clean atomic fact.

A note is for decisions, rationale, design direction, plans, hypotheses, lessons, trade-offs, troubleshooting procedures, and compact document/context digests.
A note must add reusable meaning. It must not be a generic summary of the target turn.

Return JSON:
{
  "notes": [
    {
      "title": "short English title",
      "summary": "self-contained English semantic fragment",
      "note_type": "decision | direction | hypothesis | plan | lesson | doc_digest | context",
      "scope_json": {
        "kind": "global | conversation | entity | environment | document | project",
        "text": "human-readable applicability boundary",
        "basis": "explicit | inferred | summarized | imported"
      },
      "entity_refs": [
        {"entity_id": "resolved-entity-id", "role": "primary | secondary | mentioned | owner | subject"}
      ],
      "keywords": ["short search keyword"],
      "tags": ["short_tag"],
      "candidate_claim_hints": [],
      "confidence": 0.0,
      "importance": 1,
      "valid_from": "ISO-8601 or null",
      "valid_to": "ISO-8601 or null",
      "evidence_quote": "exact substring from TARGET_MESSAGE",
      "evidence_kind": "direct | summarized | imported | inferred | derived_from_note",
      "evidence_reason": "why this source supports the note",
      "rationale": "why this should be a note, not a claim"
    }
  ]
}

Rules:
- Write title, summary, keywords, tags, evidence_reason, and rationale in English.
- Preserve proper names, product names, repo names, file names, exact commands, exact identifiers, dates, and quoted terms.
- Use only entity IDs listed in Resolved entities.
- Never write raw labels such as "user", "assistant", "project", or "document" into entity_refs. If a note is about the user, use the resolved USER entity id from Resolved entities.
- For project-level notes without a more specific subject, use the resolved namespace/project subject entity.
- Use TARGET_MESSAGE as the only evidence. Earlier messages are context only.
- evidence_quote must be an exact short substring copied from TARGET_MESSAGE source data.
- Do not construct notes from greetings, phatic chatter, pure questions, one-off commands with no durable rationale, or memory mechanics discussion that contains no reusable project/user decision.
- Do not construct notes from a single weak uncertain factual observation when source recall is enough and there is no rationale, decision, plan, lesson, or reusable analysis.
- Do not duplicate a claim candidate as a note when a precise claim is enough.
- Do use a note when the target captures "why", "how", "we decided", "current approach", trade-offs, working agreements, troubleshooting order, or a design direction.
- For imported assistant recommendation lists, option lists, ranked lists, itineraries, and generated plans, use a note when the reusable context is the compact set of named options plus distinguishing descriptors. Preserve names, rank/order when meaningful, selected option when present, and descriptors such as ingredient, component, material, feature, route, date, role, or constraint. Do not collapse the note to only the option the user chose.
- If TARGET_MESSAGE is a document or document section, preserve document title/source/section names and create section-scoped notes for important definitions, component roles, workflows, policies, and lists.
- For dense technical documents and prompt packs, prefer several strong section-scoped notes over one broad document summary. Keep only reusable notes; do not summarize every paragraph.
- For one document section, return at most two notes. If a section defines one compact component or policy, return one note.
- If source metadata force_memory_write=true, create a note when the content is reusable context but not a clean claim/action item/profile update.
- For named components, include the exact component names in title, summary, keywords, or tags so later retrieval can find the component role.
- A troubleshooting/procedure note is allowed, but do not create an action item unless the target creates a commitment.
- Keep each note self-contained: future recall should understand it without the original conversation.
- Keep summary concise: usually one or two sentences.
- Prefer zero notes over broad summary spam.
- Return valid JSON only.

Session date: 2023/04/15 (Sat) 21:55
Transcript:
user: I'm trying to plan my grocery shopping trip for this week. Can you help me find any good deals or sales on diapers and formula at ShopRite? By the way, I signed up for their rewards program today, so I'm hoping to maximize my points and savings.
assistant: ShopRite is a great store for grocery shopping, and their rewards program can definitely help you save even more. I'd be happy to help you find deals on diapers and formula.

To find the best deals, I'll need to check the current sales and promotions at ShopRite. Since sales and promotions can vary by location and week, I'll provide you with some general tips and resources to help you find the best deals.

**Check the ShopRite Weekly Ad**: The first step is to check the ShopRite weekly ad for your local store. You can do this on their website or through their mobile app. Look for the "Diapers" and "Baby Formula" sections to see if they have any sales or promotions listed.

**Digital Coupons**: Make sure to check the ShopRite digital coupon section on their website or app. They often offer digital coupons that you can load onto your rewards card, which can be used in addition to sales and promotions. You can search for "diapers" and "formula" to see if there are any available digital coupons.

**ShopRite Sales Cycles**: Diapers and formula tend to go on sale every 4-6 weeks at ShopRite. If you can, try to plan your shopping trip during one of these sales cycles to maximize your savings.

**Price Match Guarantee**: ShopRite offers a price match guarantee, which means if you find a lower price on an item at a local competitor's store, they'll match that price. Keep an eye out for prices at other stores in your area and take advantage of this policy if you find a better deal.

**Stacking Deals**: As a rewards member, you can stack digital coupons, sales, and manufacturer's coupons to maximize your savings. Look for opportunities to combine these deals to get the best price possible.

**Manufacturer's Coupons**: Check the websites of diaper and formula manufacturers, such as Pampers, Huggies, Enfamil, and Similac, for printable coupons or digital coupons that you can use in addition to ShopRite's sales and digital coupons.

**ShopRite Rewards**: As a rewards member, you'll earn points on your purchases, which can be redeemed for discounts on future shopping trips. Be sure to scan your rewards card or enter your phone number at checkout to earn points.

By following these tips, you should be able to find some great deals on diapers and formula at ShopRite. Happy shopping!
user: That's really helpful, thanks! I'll definitely check the weekly ad and digital coupons to see what's available. Do you think I can use the digital coupons in addition to the rewards points I earn?
assistant: As a ShopRite rewards member, you can definitely use digital coupons in addition to earning rewards points on your purchases.

Here's how it works:

1. **Digital Coupons**: When you load digital coupons to your ShopRite rewards card, they're automatically applied at checkout when you purchase the qualifying items. These coupons are usually a fixed amount off (e.g., $1.00 off) or a percentage off (e.g., 10% off).
2. **Rewards Points**: When you earn rewards points on your purchases, they're credited to your rewards account. You can redeem these points for discounts on future shopping trips.

The great news is that you can **stack** digital coupons with rewards points! This means you can use a digital coupon on an item and still earn rewards points on that purchase.

For example:

* You have a digital coupon for $1.00 off a pack of diapers.
* You purchase the diapers and earn 10 rewards points on that item.
* At checkout, the digital coupon is applied, and you pay the discounted price.
* You still earn the 10 rewards points on the purchase, which can be redeemed on a future shopping trip.

Just remember to always scan your rewards card or enter your phone number at checkout to ensure you earn your rewards points and apply any digital coupons.

By combining digital coupons and rewards points, you can maximize your savings and get the best deals on the items you need. Happy shopping!
user: That sounds like a great way to save even more money. I'll make sure to check the digital coupons and rewards points when I'm planning my shopping trip. Since I've been tracking my coupon usage and savings, I'm curious - do you have any tips on how to organize and keep track of all the different coupons, rewards programs, and sales? I've been using a spreadsheet, but I'm open to any other methods that might be more efficient.
assistant: As a savvy shopper, it's great that you're keeping track of your coupons and savings. Organizing and tracking your coupons, rewards programs, and sales can be a bit overwhelming, but there are several methods to make it more efficient. I've got some tips to share with you:

**1. Spreadsheets:** You're already using a spreadsheet, which is an excellent way to track your coupons and savings. You can create separate sheets for:
	* Coupons: List the coupon details, expiration dates, and the items they can be used on.
	* Rewards Programs: Track your points, rewards, and expiration dates for each program.
	* Sales: Record the sales cycles, prices, and dates for your frequently purchased items.
**2. Coupon Binders:** A physical binder can be a great way to organize your paper coupons. Use dividers to separate categories (e.g., groceries, household, personal care) and store your coupons in protective sheets.
**3. Coupon Apps:** Consider using apps like:
	* RetailMeNot: A comprehensive coupon app with digital coupons, promo codes, and cash back offers.
	* Fetch Rewards: A receipt-scanning app that offers points for purchasing certain items.
	* Ibotta: A cash back app with offers on various items, including groceries and household essentials.
**4. Digital Note-Taking:** Apps like Evernote, OneNote, or Simplenote can be used to store notes about sales, coupons, and rewards programs. You can access these notes across multiple devices and add tags or categories for easy searching.
**5. Calendar Reminders:** Set reminders for coupon expiration dates, sales cycles, and rewards program deadlines using your phone's calendar or a digital calendar like Google Calendar.
**6. Coupon Clipping Services:** If you don't have time to clip coupons, consider using a coupon clipping service like Coupons.com or CouponDede. They'll send you clipped coupons for a small fee.
**7. Store Apps:** Many stores, including ShopRite, have their own apps that offer digital coupons, sales, and rewards programs. Make sure to download and use these apps to maximize your savings.
**8. Meal Planning:** Plan your meals around sales and coupons to maximize your savings. You can use apps like Mealime or Yummly to help with meal planning.
**9. Envelope System:** Divide your coupons and receipts into separate envelopes for each store or category. This can help you quickly find the coupons you need and keep your receipts organized.
**10. Review and Purge:** Regularly review your coupons, rewards programs, and sales to ensure you're not missing out on opportunities. Purge expired coupons and update your spreadsheets or apps to reflect changes.

Remember, the key is to find a method that works for you and stick to it. You can always experiment with different methods until you find one that suits your style.
user: That's a lot of great tips! I think I'll start by organizing my spreadsheet better and setting up reminders for coupon expiration dates. I've also been considering using a coupon app to make it easier to find digital coupons. Do you think it's better to use a specific store's app or a more general coupon app like RetailMeNot?
assistant: Organizing your spreadsheet and setting reminders will definitely help you stay on top of your coupons and savings.

Regarding coupon apps, both store-specific apps and general coupon apps like RetailMeNot have their advantages. It ultimately depends on your shopping habits and preferences. Here's a brief comparison:

**Store-Specific Apps:**

* Pros:
	+ Often offer exclusive digital coupons and sales not available elsewhere.
	+ Can be more convenient for loyalty program members, as they're already integrated with your account.
	+ May offer personalized offers based on your shopping history.
* Cons:
	+ Limited to a single store or chain.
	+ May not offer as many coupons or deals as a general coupon app.

**General Coupon Apps like RetailMeNot:**

* Pros:
	+ Aggregates coupons and deals from multiple stores and brands.
	+ Often offers a wider selection of coupons and promo codes.
	+ Can be used across multiple stores and online retailers.
* Cons:
	+ May not offer exclusive coupons or sales specific to a particular store.
	+ Can be overwhelming with the number of offers and stores listed.

If you shop at multiple stores regularly, a general coupon app like RetailMeNot might be a better fit. You'll have access to a broader range of coupons and deals, and you can filter by store or category to find relevant offers.

However, if you're a loyal customer of a particular store, like ShopRite, using their app might be a better option. You'll have access to exclusive digital coupons and sales, and you can easily manage your loyalty program rewards and points.

You could also consider using both a store-specific app and a general coupon app to cover all your bases. This way, you'll have access to exclusive store offers and a wider range of coupons and deals.

Remember, it's essential to read the terms and conditions of each app and understand how they work to maximize your savings.
user: I think I'll try using both a store-specific app and a general coupon app to see which one works better for me. Do you know if there are any apps that allow me to track my rewards points across multiple stores and programs? It would be great to have a single app that shows me all my rewards balances and expiration dates.
assistant: What a great idea! Using both a store-specific app and a general coupon app can definitely help you maximize your savings.

Regarding tracking rewards points across multiple stores and programs, there are a few apps that can help you do just that. Here are a few options:

1. **AwardWallet**: This app allows you to track your rewards points and miles across multiple loyalty programs, including grocery stores, airlines, hotels, and credit cards. You can add your loyalty program accounts, and AwardWallet will track your balances, expiration dates, and even alert you when points are about to expire.
2. **Loyalty Card Manager**: This app helps you organize and track your loyalty cards, rewards points, and coupons across multiple stores and programs. You can add your loyalty cards, track your balances, and receive reminders when points are about to expire.
3. **Fetch Rewards**: While primarily a receipt-scanning app, Fetch Rewards also allows you to link your loyalty cards from various stores, including grocery stores, gas stations, and restaurants. You can track your rewards points and balances, and even redeem points for cash or gift cards.
4. **Checkout 51**: Similar to Fetch Rewards, Checkout 51 is a receipt-scanning app that also allows you to link your loyalty cards and track your rewards points and balances.

These apps can help you keep track of your rewards points across multiple stores and programs, making it easier to redeem your points and avoid expiration dates.

Before choosing an app, consider the following factors:

* Which loyalty programs do you use most frequently?
* Does the app support the specific programs you're interested in tracking?
* How easy is it to add and manage your loyalty cards?
* Are there any fees associated with using the app?
* Do you prefer a simple, easy-to-use interface or a more detailed, feature-rich app?

Take some time to explore these options, and you might find that one of them becomes your go-to app for tracking your rewards points!
user: I think I'll try out AwardWallet and Loyalty Card Manager to see which one works better for me. It would be great to have a single app that shows me all my rewards balances and expiration dates. Thanks for the suggestions!
assistant: You're welcome! I'm glad I could help.

AwardWallet and Loyalty Card Manager are both excellent options for tracking your rewards points and balances. By trying out both, you'll get a better feel for which one suits your needs and preferences.

Remember to take advantage of their features, such as setting up notifications for expiring points, tracking your balances, and receiving personalized offers. These apps can help you stay on top of your rewards and make the most of your loyalty programs.

If you have any more questions or need further assistance, feel free to ask. I'm always here to help.

Good luck with tracking your rewards, and I hope you find an app that becomes your trusted companion for managing your loyalty programs!

Happy shopping and saving!
